coredata.dc:title: Removal of uranium (VI) from water by the action of microwave-rapid green synthesized carbon quantum dots from starch-water system and supported onto polymeric matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Fekry, Nesma A.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanoparticles with superior advantages as small particle size, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity which advance their recent applications in biotechnology, bioimaging and biosensing. The use of free CQDs in water treatment is greatly rendered by their high solubility in water. Therefore, this work is aimed to rapidly synthesize CQDs in only 10 min via microwave irradiation pyrolysis of starch-water system. The maximum fluorescence emission of CQDs was detected at 526 nm throughout the excitation wavelength (390 nm). The CQDs have been targeted to occupy the surface and pores of a polymeric material based on poly(anthranilic acid-formaldehyde-phthalic acid) (PAFP) to produce a novel CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent. The surface area of CQDs@PAFP was detected (28.79 m2 g−1 BET) and the nanoparticle size was confirmed (TEM). The highest removals of U(VI) by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent were 95.5–98.0 % for 30−90 mg L-1. The sorption mechanism was designated to the pseudo-second-order model and closely tailored with Freundlich model. CQDs@PAFP was emerged as an excellent nanobiosorbent for U(VI) removal from wastewater (97.3 %) and sea water (96.0 %). CQDs@PAFP confirmed its excellent reusablity for efficient multi- recovery of U(VI) from different water samples.

head.ce:title: Removal of uranium (VI) from water by the action of microwave-rapid green synthesized carbon quantum dots from starch-water system and supported onto polymeric matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Moharem Bey, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Moharem Bey, Alexandria, Egypt, Telephone number: 002-01281716085, Fax number: 00203-3911794.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanoparticles with superior advantages as small particle size, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity which advance their recent applications in biotechnology, bioimaging and biosensing. The use of free CQDs in water treatment is greatly rendered by their high solubility in water. Therefore, this work is aimed to rapidly synthesize CQDs in only 10 min via microwave irradiation pyrolysis of starch-water system. The maximum fluorescence emission of CQDs was detected at 526 nm throughout the excitation wavelength (390 nm). The CQDs have been targeted to occupy the surface and pores of a polymeric material based on poly(anthranilic acid-formaldehyde-phthalic acid) (PAFP) to produce a novel CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent. The surface area of CQDs@PAFP was detected (28.79 m2 g−1 BET) and the nanoparticle size was confirmed (TEM). The highest removals of U(VI) by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent were 95.5–98.0 % for 30−90 mg L-1. The sorption mechanism was designated to the pseudo-second-order model and closely tailored with Freundlich model. CQDs@PAFP was emerged as an excellent nanobiosorbent for U(VI) removal from wastewater (97.3 %) and sea water (96.0 %). CQDs@PAFP confirmed its excellent reusablity for efficient multi- recovery of U(VI) from different water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the solubility problem of CQDs in water has been overcome by covalent CQDs binding to polymeric solid support (PAFP) for the formation of a novel CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent as the proposed strategy is expected to prevent water solubility of CQDs. To accomplish this target, a green-production process of CQDs from solution of starch in water has been successfully established by using simple microwave-assisted heating approach for only 10 min. The PAFP was synthesized by condensation and polymerization of anthranilic acid with formaldehyde and phthalic acid. The produced PAFP is functionalized with a number of carboxylic groups to provide excellent affinity for covalent binding with CQDs-hydroxyl groups to form an ester linkage. The structure and morphology of CQDs@PAFP were examined by FT-IR, EDX, SEM, TGA, TEM and BET surface area. The CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was targeted to apply for high-speed and selective removal of uranium ions from aqueous solution in presence of different optimized factors using microwave adsorption irradiation. The regeneration of CQDs@PAFP was also investigated in this study. Equilibrium, kinetic and thermodynamics were employed to evaluate the adsorption of U(VI) ions onto CQDs@PAFP. Finally, CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent has been potentially investigated for removal of uranium ions from three different water samples.
All chemicals and characterization devices with their specifications are listed in Table 1 .

The CQDs@PAFP was prepared by the following steps. The polymerization occurred by mixing and condensation of 0.1 mol of anthranilic acid with 0.2 mol of formaldehyde and 0.1 mol of phthalic acid in a round flask using acetic acid as a catalyst and heating till obtaining a light brown suspension. Then CQDs were added to the suspension solution of polymer and this mixture was refluxed at 140 °C for 8 h. The brown-yellowish colored precipitate was obtained, then filtrated and washed with hot water and ether. Finally, the CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was dried at 70 °C overnight. The preparation steps of this reaction are represented in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption study of U(VI) by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent

ce:section.ce:para: A standard stock solution of 1000 mg L−1 U(VI) was used to prepare 30, 60 and 90 mg L−1. The removal of U(VI) was recorded when 20 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was mixed with 20 mL of U(VI) (30, 60 and 90 mg L-1) in different pHs (1−10) already adjusted with 0.1 M NaOH or 0.1 M HCl and heating under microwave irradiation for 30 s. The concentration of U(VI) was measured by UV-spectrophotometry using l-ascorbic acid as a chromogenic reagent at λmax =360 nm ( Gupta et al., 1993 ). The percentage extraction of U(VI) by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was calculated from Eq. 1 as listed out in Table 2 .

The mass effect of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was obtained by mixing (1, 5, 10, 20, 30 and 40 mg) with 20 mL of U(VI) (30, 60 and 90 mg L−1) at pH 5.0 and heating under microwave irradiation for 30 s. The reaction mixture was filtered and the concentration of U(VI) was detected as described above. The experiments were repeated three times and the average values were computed.

The effect of competing ions was recorded by mixing 20 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent with 100 mg of interfering species (KCl, NH4Cl, NaCl, CaCl2 and MgSO4) and 20 mL of U(VI) (30, 60 and 90 mg L−1) at pH 5.0 and heating under microwave irradiation for 30 s. The reaction mixture was filtered and the concentration of U(VI) was detected as described above. The experiments were repeated three times and the average values were computed.

The point of zero charge (pHPZC) of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was determined by salt addition method. 0.2 g of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was added to 40 mL of 0.01 mol L−1 NaCl. The pHinitial (pHi) of this mixture was adjusted from 2 to 10 by adding 0.1 mol L−1 HCl or 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH. These mixtures were shaken by shaken for 4 h at room temperature. The pHfinal value of each suspension was noted after 24 h of equilibration. The (pHPZC) of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was determined by plotting ΔpH (pHfinal - pHinitial) versus pHi ( Mahmoud et al., 2020a ).

The adsorption kinetic experiments were achieved to estimate the adsorption rate and mechanism of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent. Typically, 20 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was added to 20 mL of U(VI) (30, 60 and 90 mg L−1) at pH 5.0 and heating under microwave irradiation for different times (1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 s). The concentration of U(VI) was then determined. To evaluate the rate of adsorption of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent, pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second order, intraparticle diffusion and Elovich models were applied to estimate the required data and parameters. More definition and parameter details about the kinetic models are presented in Table 2.

To achieve the adsorption isotherms, 20 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was added in 20 mL of different concentrations (20, 30, 40, 60, 80, 90, 100, 120, 130, 140, 150) mg L−1 of U(VI) at pH 5.0 and heating under microwave irradiation for 30 s. The isotherm models were fitted by using a nonlinear method, with consecutive interactions deliberated by the route of Levenberg-Marquardt. Interactions were also designed by the Simplex route, utilizing the nonlinear suitable facilities of the software Origin Pro 8.5. Moreover, the nonlinear models were also estimated by the adjusted determination operator (R2 adj) and Standard deviation (SD) ( Machado et al., 2016; Leite et al., 2017; Kasperiski et al., 2018a, 2018b ) to gauge the change in the amount of U(VI) adsorbed onto the CQDs@PAFP as predicted by the nonlinear models with the experimentally determined q-value. The R2 adj and SD values are given by Eqs. (10) and (11) as listed in Table 2.

To obtain the thermodynamic parameters, 20 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was added in 20 mL of U(VI) (30, 60 and 90 mg L−1) with different concentrations at various temperatures (293, 309, 333 and 343 K) by changing the power of microwave at pH 5.0 for 30 s. The best fitted isotherm model and data were used to calculate the values of Gibbs free energy, enthalpy and entropy. Thermodynamic parameters were applied to identify the affinity of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent for U(VI). The parameters can be estimated from the equations listed in Table 2.

To evaluate the regeneration process of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent, 0.5 g of the nanobiosorbent was mixed with 200 mL of U(VI) (30 mg L−1) and heated in a microwave oven for 60 s. Then, the desorption agent (100 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 HCl) was added on U(VI)-CQDs@PAFP under microwave heating for 60 s. The recycled CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was further used for U(VI) ions removal from aqueous solution. All steps were repeated for five cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent for U(VI) removal
ce:section.ce:para: The microwave adsorption technique was applied by using CQDs@PAFP as an excellent nanobiosorbent for removal of U(VI) from tap water, sea water and wastewater. The pH values of investigated samples were initially measured and found to correspond to 6.98, 8.65 and 7.71 in tap water, sea water and wastewater, respectively. These samples were adjusted to pH 5.0 before spiking the required U(VI) concentration. The removal experiments were conducted by mixing 50 mg of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent with 250 mL of 20 mg L−1 U(VI) spiked in different samples of water. Then, the mixture solution was irradiated under microwave at 60 s. Finally, the percentage of U(VI) removal was determined by using Eq.1 as given in Table 2.

Independent two-sample t-test (origin pro 8.5 software) was accomplished at confidence interval (95 %) between the replicate values achieved through the experimental tests to decide whether or not the values of replication were significantly different. To confirm the optimum values of pH, CQDs@PAFP dosage, and initial U(VI) concentration for maximum removal percentage of U(VI), a test of one-way (ANOVA) was shown at confidence interval (95 %). In addition, this test was also implemented to detect the effect of initial U(VI) concentration on the capacity of CQDs@PAFP in adsorption process. Mean difference Tukey test (MDT) was calculated for several comparisons when the ANOVA presented significant differences (p-value < 0.05).

The morphology of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was explained by SEM. In Fig. 2 (a), the shape of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent looks like a flower or leaves of a tree. The particle size of CQDs@PAFP was between 35.21–73.11 nm. The SEM image shows more pores on the surface in CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent that can act as the active site and good monodispersity for enhancing sorption process of U(VI) ions.

The elemental arrangement was determined from the EDX diagram (Fig. 4 ). The EDX of CQDs refers to 43.52 % carbon and 56.48 % oxygen (Fig. 4a). CQDs@PAFP before and after the adsorption of U(VI) are shown in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. The diagram confirms the successful formation of heterostructure CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent. Fig. 4c shows the presence of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP surface after adsorption process to confirm good adsorption ability of CQDs@PAFP for U(VI) ions. In addition, the U(VI) content was increased to give 23.30 %, while the carbon, nitrogen and oxygen percentage values were decreased to 48.43 %, 4.99 % and 23.28 %, respectively upon loading U(VI) ions onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent. Therefore, it is evident that the C-, N- and O- containing functional groups on CQDs@PAFP were successfully contributed to U(VI) adsorption process.

According to the BET method, the surface area of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was appraised to be 28.79 m2 g−1. The CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent showed total pore volume = 0.099 cm3. g−1. In Fig. 5 a, the desorption/adsorption of nitrogen on CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent is considered a type IV isotherm, which characteristically of mesoporous materials, with a type H3 hysteresis associated to slit or plate shaped pores ( Ibrahim et al., 2016 ). The mean pore diameter of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was found 13.79 nm to refer to mesoporous structure. Although the surface area is small, the coating of CQDs as low size particles on the surface of PAFP resulted in fabrication of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent with a high number of available functional groups to improve its adsorption capability for binding with U(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pH on removal U(VI) by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent
ce:section.ce:para: As pH can influence the surface charge of CQDs@PAFP, active sites of nanobiosorbent and also the chemistry of U(VI), the pH is considered to be one of the most important factors affecting adsorption process. The power of pH on U(VI) removal is depicted in Fig. 6 a. The factor of pH was investigated using three different U(VI) concentrations (30 mg L−1, 60 mg L−1, 90 mg L−1). In Fig. 6a, the extraction behavior of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent could be described by three steps. The uptake amount of U(VI) gradually increased from pH < 4.0 (step I), rapidly increased between 3.0 < pH < 6.0 (step II) and then decreased at pH > 7.0 (step III). In step I, U(VI) ions existed in the positively charged form (UO2 2+), so the repulsion occurs between (UO2 2+) ions and the positively charged surface of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent ( Yao et al., 2018; Basu et al., 2015 ). However, it was value pointing out that even when the CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent surface was positively charged, the CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent still exhibit a relative high percentage of extraction to refer to the possible binding between (UO2 2+) ions and surface nitrogen and/or oxygen containing active sites. At pH 1.0, the extraction of U(VI) ions was identified as 36.5 %, 43.5 % and 56.1 % for 30, 60, 90 mg L−1, respectively. In step II, upon increase of pH value from 4.0–5.0, the percentage removal values of U(VI) were also increased (85.9 % and 95.5 % for 30 mg L−1), (87.5 % and 96.8 % for 60 mg L−1), (90.2 % and 98.0 % for 90 mg L−1), respectively. Thus, the decreasing of positive charge on U(VI) from two (UO2 2+) to one (UO2)3(OH)5 + and (UO2)4(OH)7)+ which could lower repulsion than in step I. The highest percentages of U(VI) were 95.5 %, 96.8 % and 98.1 % for 30, 60 and 90 mg L−1, respectively were observed at pH 5.0. In step III, the low adsorption percentages were mainly accredited to strong repulsion between negatively charged U(VI) species (UO2)3(OH)7)-, UO2(OH)4 2- and UO2(OH)3)- and negatively charged active sites on CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent (Yao et al., 2018).

In ANOVA test, the results of percentage U(VI) removal was significantly different by various pH (1−10) (P-value > 0.05) at different concentrations. According to Tukey test, this test was accomplished for comparison between different pHs. The removal percentage of U(VI) was not significantly different between different pHs except 4.0 vs 1.0–2.0, 5.0 vs 1.0–3.0 and 7.0–10.0, 6.0 vs 1.0–2.0, 9.0 vs 4.0 and 10.0 vs 4.0−6.0. In Fig. 6a, the maximum removal U(VI) was obtained at pH 5.0 and also Tukey test designated that the U(VI) removal at pH 5.0 is significantly different than other values of pHs. Therefore, pH 5.0 can be deliberated as the optimum pH for removal of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent mass on removal of U(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: In ANOVA test, the results of U(VI) removal percentage were significantly different at various masses (1−40 mg) (P-value < 0.05) at different concentrations. By Tukey test, it was confirmed that the percentage removal of U(VI) is significantly different at various CQDs@PAFP dosages except the difference between 10 and 5 mg, 20 and 30 mg, 20 and 40 mg as well as 30 and 40 mg. There is no significant U(VI) removal difference between 20 mg and (30 & 40 mg) of CQDs@PAFP dosage. Therefore, CQDs@PAFP dosage of 20 mg can be deliberated as an optimum dosage.

The percentage of U(VI) extraction onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent as a function of time is shown in Fig. 6c. It was found that the (%) removal of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent increased rapidly from 1 s to 15 s, then increased slowly from 15 s to 30 s till reach equilibrium. The ANOVA test confirmed that the U(VI) removal percentage was significant (p-value < 0.05). Even though there were no significant difference in U(VI) removal percentages between 25 s and 30 s, 25 s and 20 s, 25 s and 15 s, 30 s and 20 s as well as 20 s and 15 s, indicating that the equilibrium was reached after 10 s. The initial percentage U(VI) removal onto CQDs@PAFP (at 1 s) was 45.9 % (30 mg L−1), 55.0 % (60 mg L−1) and 55.6 % (90 mg L−1) and these values reached to the maximum at 30 s as 95.0 % (30 mg L−1), 96.8 % (60 mg L−1) and 98.04 % (90 mg L−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption isotherm of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent
ce:section.ce:para: This study has been designed to evaluate the maximum capacities of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent by using different U(VI) concentrations (20−150 mg L−1) at pH 5.0 and 30 s microwave heating until equilibrium was established from 293 K to 343 K. The capacity of U(VI) extraction by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was perceived to increase with the increment in U(VI) ions until it reached the maximum capacities (130.1 mg g-1) at 293 K, (135.5 mg g-1) at 309 K, (140.1 mg g-1) at 333 K and (147.6 mg g-1) at 343 K. As given in Table 4 , CQDs@PAFP exhibited an excellent capacity for U(VI) ions (147.6 mg g−1) when compared with other adsorbents.

The sorption equilibrium by CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was described by four nonlinear models (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and D–R). The Langmuir is used to describe adsorption as monolayer, where the activation energy of adsorption is uniform on the surface of adsorbent. While, the Freundlich model was used to designate the adsorption process onto heterogeneous surfaces. Temkin model was employed to describe the interactions between the adsorbent (CQDs@PAFP) and adsorbate (U(VI)). This model assumed that the temperature of all molecules during adsorption decrease when the surface of adsorbent is more covered by adsorbate. D–R model is an empirical model to describe mechanism of adsorption with free energy onto heterogeneous of adsorbent surfaces (porosity). The evaluation of equilibrium data of U(VI) ions by the statistical analysis were achieved through R2 Adj and SD values, which are provided in Table 5 . According to the R2 Adj and SD values of U(VI), the Freundlich model is the best model for adsorption of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP at different temperatures. The Freundlich model presented the highest R2 Adj and lowest SD at various temperatures. So, the sorption characteristics of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent followed more closely the Freundlich model. At all of three temperatures, comparing the n-values were found smaller than one and this refers to a favorable cooperative adsorption ( Hameed et al., 2008 ). Moreover, CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent appeared to be covered by a widespread variety of pore size and pore volume distributions with the presence of totally different functionalities like -NH2, COOH, OH groups which ultimately possess the characteristic merits of random and heterogeneous surface. These functional groups on the surface of CQDs@PAFP could form stable complexes with U(VI) as shown as Scheme 2 .

In Langmuir model, the lowest value of R2 Adj and highest SD are indicating that the adsorption behavior is not following this model. This reflection is further maintained by the assessment of the respective R2 Adj, which is a gauge of how well the predicated values can estimate model contest with the investigational data. In Temkin model, the value of bT and R2 were 13.98 J. mol−1 and 0.9606, respectively. This indicates that the adsorption behavior is not following this model. Finally, the Kad and qs values were 180 mol2 K J2 and 515.43 mg g−1, respectively using the D–R model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regeneration and reusability of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent
Application of CQDs@PAFP for U(VI) ions removal from different water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent for removal of U(VI) ions from real water samples is a very important step that must be evaluated in this study. The validity of using CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent for removal of U(VI) from seawater is also an important issue. So, CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was proven with excellent applicability for removal of U(VI) from various samples of water at a short time (60 s) by microwave sorption technique. The extraction results for recovery of U(VI) were found to correspond to 97.3 %, 96.0 % and 80.9 % from wastewater, sea water and tap water, respectively to confirm the potential and validation of the designed CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been successfully synthesized via green-production from of starch-water system by using rapid microwave-assisted heating approach for only 10 min. CQDs were then used to develop a novel nanobiosorbent via supporting on PAFP polymeric matrix to produce CQDs@PAFP. The EDX analysis of U(VI)-CQDs@PAFP material confirmed the capability of assembled CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent for adsorptive removal of U(VI) from aqueous solution. Owing to the availability of various surface functional groups, the investigated CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent exhibited excellent adsorption and high selectivity toward U(VI) ions at short microwave heating time (30 s). The adsorption isotherm of U(VI) ions onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent was found appropriate by Freundlich model. The kinetics of U(VI) adsorption onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent followed the pseudo-second order model to assure the chemical adsorption in spontaneous mode. Using shorter regeneration time, the recycled CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent showed excellent and cost-effective capability for removal of U(VI) from aqueous solution. Finally, upon microwave heating for 60 s, the percentage extractions of U(VI) were identified as 97.3 % and 96 % from wastewater and sea water samples, respectively. However, low concentration levels of U(VI) (ppb or less) in some environmental samples will be investigated in the future work.

Mohamed E. Mahmoud: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Nesma A. Fekry: Data curation, Software, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Amir M. Abdelfattah: Supervision, Investigation, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
